chapter_title: Type Conversion, Type Aliases, and Type Deduction
lesson_title: Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.front: What is type deduction in C++?
cards.back: A feature where the compiler automatically deduces (figures out) the type of an object from its initializer. The auto keyword enables type deduction for variables.
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.text: To use type deduction for a variable, use the {{c1::auto}} keyword instead of an explicit type: auto x = 5;
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.question: What type does the variable d get?
cards.explanation: The compiler deduces the type from the initializer. 5.0 is a double literal, so d has type double.
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.front: Can you use auto without an initializer?
cards.back:
No. Variables using auto MUST be initialized at definition because the compiler needs the initializer to deduce the type.
auto x; // Error!
auto y = 5; // OK
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.text: When using auto, the variable {{c1::must be initialized}} at definition because the compiler needs the {{c2::initializer}} to deduce the type.
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.explanation:
Using auto provides several benefits:
  • Reduces typing for complex type names
  • Avoids type mismatches and implicit conversions
  • Easier refactoring (change return type, auto adapts)
  • Can't forget to initialize (requires initializer)
  • Makes code more maintainable
However, can reduce readability if type isn't obvious from context.
examples: auto result = findStudent(id);  // don't need to know return type
auto iter = myMap.begin();      // much shorter than iterator type
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.front: What happens to const and references when using auto?
cards.back:
auto drops const and reference qualifiers by default.
  • const int → auto deduces int
  • int& → auto deduces int
  • const int& → auto deduces int
To preserve them, use auto const, auto&, or auto const&.
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.question: What types do these variables get?
cards.code:
const int x{ 5 };
auto a{ x };       // 1
const auto b{ x }; // 2
auto& c{ x };      // 3
cards.answer:
a: int (drops const)
b: const int (explicitly adds const)
c: const int& (reference to const)
cards.explanation: auto drops const by default. To preserve const, you must add it explicitly. References are preserved when using auto&.
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.text: auto drops {{c1::const}} and {{c2::reference}} qualifiers by default. To preserve them, use {{c3::const auto}} or {{c4::auto&}}.
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.front: When should you avoid using auto?
cards.back:
Avoid auto when:
  • The type is not obvious from context
  • You want to be explicit about the type for clarity
  • You specifically want a different type than what would be deduced
  • Documentation/readability would suffer
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.question: What types are deduced?
cards.code:
auto a{ 5 };     // 1
auto b{ 5.0 };   // 2
auto c{ 5.0f };  // 3
auto d{ 'x' };   // 4
cards.explanation: auto deduces types based on literal suffixes: no suffix = int for integers, double for decimals; f suffix = float; char literals = char.
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.front: What type does auto deduce for a string literal?
cards.back:
const char* (pointer to const char), NOT std::string.
auto s = "hello"; // const char*
To get std::string, use: auto s = std::string{"hello"};
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.text: The {{c1::auto}} keyword enables {{c2::type deduction}}, allowing the compiler to deduce a variable's type from its {{c3::initializer}}.
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword

cards.text: To preserve const when using auto, write {{c1::const auto}}. To preserve references, write {{c2::auto&}}.
cards.source: 10.8 - Type deduction for objects using the auto keyword
